title: Dog Head Tilt Guide: Causes, Urgency, and Veterinary Triage
meta_desc: Is your dog's head tilted? Learn about vestibular disease, ear infections, and when a head tilt requires an urgent trip to the veterinarian.
content_html:
What is a Head Tilt in Dogs?

A head tilt occurs when a dog holds their head slanted to one side, often looking as though they are listening intently. While it can appear subtle, it is typically a clinical sign of a disturbance in the vestibular system, which is responsible for maintaining balance and spatial orientation. This system involves components in both the inner ear and the brain.

Is it an Emergency?

The urgency level for a sudden head tilt is classified as Medium. While a head tilt is rarely a life-threatening emergency on its own, it usually indicates discomfort, vertigo, or an underlying infection that needs to be addressed within 24 hours. If your dog is also experiencing extreme lethargy, an inability to stand, or persistent vomiting, you should contact an emergency clinic immediately.

Why a Photo or Video Helps Triage

Providing your veterinarian with a photo or a short video of your dog's head tilt is incredibly valuable for triage. It allows the medical team to see the exact angle of the tilt and observe any accompanying signs, such as nystagmus (rapid, involuntary eye movements) or unsteadiness in their gait. These visual cues help the vet determine the severity of the balance issue and prioritize your pet's appointment accordingly.

faqs.question: Can an ear infection cause a head tilt?
faqs.answer: Yes, an infection that moves into the middle or inner ear can disrupt balance and result in a persistent head tilt.

faqs.question: What is 'Old Dog' Vestibular Disease?
faqs.answer: Also known as Idiopathic Vestibular Disease, this is a common condition in senior dogs that causes sudden dizziness and a head tilt, often improving on its own with supportive care.

faqs.question: Will a head tilt go away on its own?
faqs.answer: While some cases resolve without specific treatment, most require a veterinary exam to treat the root cause, such as polyps, infections, or neurological issues.
